nodes.title: The Routledge Handbook of Translation and Methodology

deep_analysis.title: The Routledge Handbook of Translation and Methodology
deep_analysis.problem: The challenge of managing and accessing large volumes of linguistic data for corpus-based translation studies and practical translation tasks.
deep_analysis.method:
Development and use of corpus management tools such as Sketch Engine and modnlp/tec, which provide capabilities for tokenisation, indexing, metadata management, and data presentation.

**Explanation:** These tools facilitate the organization, retrieval, and manipulation of linguistic data, making it easier for researchers and translators to conduct detailed and scalable analyses without being limited by accessibility issues or software constraints. For instance, modnlp/tec allows for both local and remote access to corpora, handling metadata in XML format, which helps manage complex linguistic datasets effectively.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The absence of a stand-alone, offline tool limits the flexibility for experienced users, as they might not have the legal right to share their resources or could face issues if web-based tools are withdrawn or changed.
- Ensuring the proper use of machine learning models and algorithms within translation studies requires a level of proficiency that cannot be achieved simply through better user interfaces, posing a challenge for users lacking expertise in these methods.
- There is a need for interdisciplinary collaboration to make further methodological progress in emerging fields such as corpus-based translation studies, implying that current methods alone are insufficient without such joint efforts.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Develop new techniques for visualizing and explaining machine learning models to improve their interpretability and ensure proper use in translation studies.
- Create standalone, offline tools with open-source licensing to increase flexibility and reproducibility for experienced users in computational linguistics and corpus-based studies.
- Encourage interdisciplinary collaboration among translation scholars, linguists, statisticians, and computer scientists to enhance methodology in corpus-based translation studies.
- Monitor advancements in text visualization technology as a means to complement computational linguistics methods and improve user interfaces and experiences in translation processes.

problem_evidence.text: Computational linguistic methods can be categorised as symbolic methods and statistical learning methods... The modnlp software suite runs on most computer operating systems, and has been used extensively in translation studies.

method_evidence.text: Computational linguistic methods can be categorised as symbolic methods and statistical learning methods... The modnlp software suite runs on most computer operating systems, and has been used extensively in translation studies.

limitation_evidence.text: Clearly, good user interface support for users of computational linguistics tools is crucial in applications such as translator training and computer assisted translation. With the advent of corpus based translation studies, it has also become important to provide the research community with usable tools which will also allow researchers to document and share their work. Tools such as the Sketch Engine (Kilgarriff et al. 2014) , the BYU corpora (Davies and Fuchs 2015; Davies 2010) , the CQPweb corpus analysis system (Hardie 2012) , and the modnlp/tec (Luz 2011 ) software suite can be seen as efforts towards these goals. However, the use of analytic tools and corpora is still hampered by software access and licensing constraints. With the exception of modnlp/tec, all of the above mentioned tools are exclusively web based. While using the web as a platform certainly facilitates access, the absence of a stand-alone, offline tool limits the more experienced user's flexibility and their ability to explore their own resources, which they might not wish or have the legal right to share. Another issue of access concerns licensing terms which might limit access to software source code, or prevent it entirely. Unfortunately, most web based tools in this area are commercial products, provide very limited functionality, or charge fees for "premium access". Two exceptions among the tools cited are CQPweb and modnlp/tec, both of which are distributed under FLOSS licenses. The ability to inspect and modify source code has been increasingly regarded as a crucial aspect of reproducibility in data-intensive research (Hutson 2018) . If corpus based studies are to develop a robust methodology for the use of computational linguistics tools and methods, the issue of sharing source code as well as data needs to be addressed. A related issue is the availability and stability of the software. Users of purely web based tools are entirely dependent on the software provider for their analytic work. If the tool's underlying algorithms changes, or the tool is withdrawn from public access, the corpus scholar potentially faces the situation of having their analyses invalidated (in the case of algorithm changes) or uncorroborated (in the case of access withdrawal). While several web-based text visualisation tools have appeared recently foot_4 , these Ideally, standardised, FLOSS platforms will be built in the future which allow corpus and translation researchers to document and share their analyses, perhaps along the lines of what has been done for "vernacular visualisation" (Viégas and Wattenberg 2008) , where users are encouraged to produce, document and share their analyses.

As machine learning models and algorithms start to become part of the translator's and translator scholar's toolbox, the issue arises of ensuring that these models and algorithms are well understood and properly used. This is not a trivial issue, witness the concerns in other traditionally data-intensive research communities regarding the misuses and misinterpretations of simple statistical tests. As machine learning models tend to be a lot more complex, and in many cases more opaque than the models used in traditional statistical testing, their potential misuse should be a cause for concern. For instance, while the interpretation of a co-occurrence matrix is straightforward, an embedding vector which might involve non-linear transformations of the original data would not be as easy to interpret. Therefore, somewhat in opposition to the need to provide usable interfaces just discussed, it seems that ensuring the proper use of computational linguistics methods based on machine learning will require a level of proficiency in these methods which cannot be achieved simply through better user interfaces. This is however an active area of research, and new techniques of visualisation and model explanation are being proposed which might alleviate this problem in future. A related issue concerns the incorporation of new technology into translator education. Doherty and Kenny (2014) argue for ways of incorporating statistical machine translation into the translation training curriculum in a way that promotes a good understanding of the underlying technology and therefore empowers the translator.

From education to scholarly studies, it is clear that the arrival of new language technologies has disrupted established practices, so that emerging fields such as corpus-based translation studies now find themselves at a stage where further progress in methodology can only be made through the joint efforts of researchers from several disciplines, including translation scholars, linguists, statisticians and computer scientists. This state of affairs has been anticipated to some extent (Baker 2000) , but as the field evolves the need for interdisciplinary collaboration becomes more evident.

As computational linguistics and machine learning methods make further inroads into translation and translation studies, potential issues arise which need to be examined from a methodological perspective. These issues include: the need to support the translator and the translation scholar with tools and user interfaces that enable usable and effective access to large volumes of text and facilitate selection and comparison of sub-corpora, the interpretability of the statistical and connectionist models employed, and issues concerning the generation and validation of hypotheses and conclusions reached through the use of computational linguistics methods.

• Monitor the literature for new developments in text visualisation technology. This is a fast-moving field which is gaining increasing importance with the widespread use of neural networks and other machine learning models in computational linguistics. Text visualisation tools can act as an effective complement to computatinal linguistics methods. For the translation researcher, this is important as visualisation tool may help the user gain an accurate overview of the data and formulate research hypotheses. For the practitioner, these tools have the potential to improve user interface, and the user's overall experience of the translation process.

nodes.title: Natural Language Processing: Structure and Complexity

deep_analysis.title: Natural Language Processing: Structure and Complexity
deep_analysis.problem: There is a lack of quantitative measures to predict and manage the complexity of natural language processing (NLP) tasks before building applications, leading to potential inefficiencies and unfeasibility in NLP projects.
deep_analysis.method:
The authors propose using a modified Kolmogorov complexity approach, specifically the concept of Q-complexity, which utilizes meaning automata to measure and analyze the complexity of NLP tasks.

**Explanation:** By defining semantic complexity through a set of questions that an NLP system can answer, the approach allows for a numerical estimation of task difficulty. This enables task division into manageable subtasks based on complexity and supports informed decision-making about the feasibility of NLP systems before development. The approach leverages theoretical foundations in Kolmogorov complexity to provide invariant complexity assessments based on the task requirements.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- Our method cannot provide exact predictions of complexity for NLP programs; the measures are estimations based on mathematical models and are only accurate if the models closely match reality.
- The paper represents only an initial step towards making quantitative assessments of NLP systems' difficulty; significant questions remain open, such as determining the size of NL grammar per task and measuring complexity across different architectures.
- The methodology requires choosing the right representations and architectures to manage complexity effectively, which suggests that the method is still limited by the potential variability and complexity introduced by different architectural choices.
- There is an inherent limitation in our approach regarding user interaction, as it requires defining the boundary of acceptable topics and controlling dialog based on user intentions, which might not anticipate all possible user queries or interactions.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Develop methods to estimate the grammar size for NLP tasks based on domain-specific concepts and required processing accuracy, referencing Zipf's laws as a potential analytical tool.
- Investigate strategies to measure NLP task complexity using different architectures, such as finite state automata or traditional linguistic layers, to better assess the difficulty of building NLP systems.
- Explore methods to control semantic complexity by limiting user interaction to predefined sets of questions, particularly in manageable domains like calendar actions.
- Assess the potential relevance of NLP complexity assessment methods for software engineering, suggesting an interdisciplinary approach to understanding semantic and grammatical complexities.

problem_evidence.text: Abstract: 'Our complexity measures are derived from the Kolmogorov complexity of a class of automata - meaning automata, whose purpose is to extract relevant pieces of information from sentences.'

method_evidence.text: Abstract: 'Our complexity measures are derived from the Kolmogorov complexity of a class of automata - meaning automata, whose purpose is to extract relevant pieces of information from sentences.'

limitation_evidence.text: Finally, it should be noted that although the tools we have introduced to analyze problems allow us to estimate the complexity of NLP programs in new domains, we cannot make exact predictions of their complexity. The complexity measures apply to mathematical models, and they come close to the reality only if those models closely resemble the reality. This situation is an exact parallel of other engineering disciplines.

However, the paper is only the first step in the direction of making quantitative assessments of the difficulty of building NLP systems; and many of natural important questions remain open. For instance, the question about the size of the NL grammar per task; or how to measure the complexity of NLP tasks based on different architectures, such as a cascade of finite state automata, or the traditional morphology-syntax-semantics-pragmatics. The final point in our discussion of the methods introduced in the paper is their possible relevance for software engineering. Here we would like to make the following points.

Architectures are there to resolve the constraints of representation, complexity, and performance; e.g. there are trade-offs between the pipeline architecture for NLP and the integrated syntax and semantics. The choice of right representations (abstract structures) allows us to keep the value of A smaller than 10K even if the complexity C of the whole program is greater than 100M states. Thus the management of complexity requires thinking about the best architecture. Furthermore, since it is possible to find examples where small differences in D may correspond to large differences in B and C (consider e.g. the difficulty of improving accuracy of a search engine or a speech recognition program), the management of the expectations of users and a clear definition of the task is of great importance.

The next element has to do with the user understanding the capabilities of the system. This includes, in its static part, defining for the user the range of acceptable topics and the types of NL constructions, e.g. by forbidding all "why" questions. The dynamic part requires the recognition and control of the borderline between what is acceptable and what is not. For instance, a meeting scheduling system can recognize a question about a past room assignment only to reply that it does not keep the database of old assignments. The dynamic part must thus include the recognition of the intentions of the user, and for dialog systems, the control of dialog (e.g. asking questions that require simple yes/no replies).

We have introduced a methodology for predicting the difficulty of coding new natural language processing programs and for analyzing existing ones. Our methods can also be used in managing complexity of NLP applications by guiding possible reformulations of the tasks.

future_work_evidence.text: Zipf's laws appear in other situations and domains, including software engineering (e.g. [20] [14] ). Furthermore, they apply both to syntactic and semantic classifications. Thus we can conjecture that they would apply to English constructions in restricted domains.

Under these assumptions it should be possible to estimate the size of the grammar for a NLP task given the number of concepts in the task domain, required accuracy of processing, and some domain-independent language parameters. How exactly one would do it seems to be an open problem, but Chapter 3 of [20] seems like a natural starting point.

future_work_evidence.section: A comparison of two dialog systems
future_work_evidence.text: In contrast, the Q-complexity of mincal is less than 1, 000 (measured again by the number of facts), there are no restrictions on the number of exchanges; but there is an implicit assumption that all dialogs are restricted to the tasks of scheduling, canceling and moving meetings, and there is no expectation of discussing the content of the meetings with the machine (the meetings could have topics though). Here, the set Q consists of questions about parameters of calendar events: action name?, event time?, ....

future_work_evidence.section: A comparison of two dialog systems
future_work_evidence.text: Thus, the domain of calendar action is manageable, the domain of divorce not. Because the control programs for both domains are of roughly the same complexity, this difference has to attributed to the size of the required database of background facts. The key to controlling the semantic complexity lies in limiting the interaction with the user to a clearly defined set of (wh-) questions. And this is the topic of the next section.

However, the paper is only the first step in the direction of making quantitative assessments of the difficulty of building NLP systems; and many of natural important questions remain open. For instance, the question about the size of the NL grammar per task; or how to measure the complexity of NLP tasks based on different architectures, such as a cascade of finite state automata, or the traditional morphology-syntax-semantics-pragmatics. The final point in our discussion of the methods introduced in the paper is their possible relevance for software engineering. Here we would like to make the following points.

nodes.title: Natural Language Processing 4 All (NLP4All): A New Online Platform for Teaching and Learning NLP Concepts

deep_analysis.title: Natural Language Processing 4 All (NLP4All): A New Online Platform for Teaching and Learning NLP Concepts
deep_analysis.problem: 缺乏编码技能和跨学科对自然语言处理方法的理解限制了学生和一般人群参与和使用自然语言处理工具。
deep_analysis.method:
提供一个名为NLP4All的在线平台，通过易于使用的界面，提供NLP方法、数据和分析，使非程序员和初学者能够互动学习NLP概念。

**Explanation:** NLP4All平台没有编程要求，允许用户在课堂环境中进行实际的NLP实验，比如文本分类。不需要复杂的编程知识，它利用用户友好的界面来引导学生通过实际数据进行实验学习。这不仅降低了技术进入的门槛，还通过互动和可视化帮助学生理解NLP模型的工作原理和应用，进而扩大了自然语言处理方法的使用范围。
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The platform currently lacks the ability to report on concrete findings or analyses of learning data, which limits its utility in assessing the effectiveness of the learning model in real-world applications.
- The current testing and implementation of NLP4All were not focused on teaching NLP itself, which may limit its comprehensiveness in developing technical skills or understanding of NLP for students interested in computer science or machine learning.
- There was an absence of detailed information regarding how the platform handles student feedback and adapts to different learning preferences or backgrounds, which could restrict its ability to personalize the educational experience.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Extend the NLP4All platform to include learning modules on tf-idf, vector-based representations, topic modeling, and word embeddings, using the resources from the grant received.
- Explore and analyze the learning data from the classroom tests after their review is completed to provide insights into the effectiveness of NLP4All in educational settings.
- Investigate the impact of using NLP methods for evidence-based analysis in social studies classes to enhance students' ability to engage in discussions with concrete data.

problem_evidence.text: 『NLP4All is an interactive tool which is designed to supplement classroom modules introducing NLP or machine learning concepts for non-specialists.』

method_evidence.text: 『NLP4All is an interactive tool which is designed to supplement classroom modules introducing NLP or machine learning concepts for non-specialists.』

limitation_evidence.section: Facilitating social studies discussion in a Danish high school
limitation_evidence.text: We tested NLP4All in a Social Studies high school classroom. In collaboration with a social studies teacher, we developed a 6-hour learning unit on language, ideology and political parties. The unit was designed to address one of learning goals of our national learning standards, specifying that students should learn about the different policy positions of political parties (we have 13 in our national parliament.) In other words, the purpose was not to teach NLP, but to teach with NLP, and to offer NLPmethods as a way of analyzing larger amounts of text than is otherwise possible. 24 2nd year (sophomore) Danish high school students participated, with roughly equal numbers of girls and boys. In a survey sent out to students prior to our test, none of these students self-reported as having any programming experience, and 20 out of 24 reported no or low interest in computer science or machine learning. All had self-selected into "Alevel" Social Studies, a 3-year elective class. About one third of students had immigrant backgrounds, slightly above the national average.

limitation_evidence.section: Facilitating social studies discussion in a Danish high school
limitation_evidence.text: The teacher and students used NLP4All to discuss tweets from pairs of Danish political parties. First, students had to label tweets and a model to tell a socialist and a nationalist party apart. Then, students did the same with the same socialist party, and a libertarian party.

limitation_evidence.section: Facilitating social studies discussion in a Danish high school
limitation_evidence.text: We cannot report on more concrete findings or analyses of learning data at present, as these results are currently under review at another venue. However, in evaluations the students reported enjoying being able to provide concrete evidence for their analyses. To them, purely qualitative analyses sometimes feel fluffy, but by showing that their analyses were backed up by hundreds or thousands of tweets made them feel more comfortable making claims during classroom discussions.

In a survey sent out to students prior to our test, none of these students self-reported as having any programming experience, and 20 out of 24 reported no or low interest in computer science or machine learning. All had self-selected into "Alevel" Social Studies, a 3-year elective class. About one third of students had immigrant backgrounds, slightly above the national average.

future_work_evidence.text: The teacher and students used NLP4All to discuss tweets from pairs of Danish political parties. First, students had to label tweets and a model to tell a socialist and a nationalist party apart. Then, students did the same with the same socialist party, and a libertarian party.

future_work_evidence.section: Facilitating social studies discussion in a Danish high school
future_work_evidence.text: We cannot report on more concrete findings or analyses of learning data at present, as these results are currently under review at another venue. However, in evaluations the students reported enjoying being able to provide concrete evidence for their analyses. To them, purely qualitative analyses sometimes feel fluffy, but by showing that their analyses were backed up by hundreds or thousands of tweets made them feel more comfortable making claims during classroom discussions.

At present, NLP4All provides support for teaching the following technical topics, without requiring any programming on the part of teachers or students: With a grant received in Spring 2021, the platform will be extended to support new learning modules on tf-idf, vector-based representations of texts, topic modeling, and word embeddings.

- There was an absence of detailed information regarding how the platform handles student feedback and adapts to different learning preferences or backgrounds, which could restrict its ability to personalize the educational experience.

nodes.title: Categorical Tools for Natural Language Processing

deep_analysis.title: Categorical Tools for Natural Language Processing
deep_analysis.problem: The challenge of creating a unified framework for syntax, semantics, and pragmatics in natural language processing.
deep_analysis.method:
Categorical tools using category theory to model syntax as string diagrams, semantics using functors, and pragmatics as compositional models.

**Explanation:** Category theory offers a structured approach to model linguistic reasoning and computation. Syntax is represented with string diagrams that provide a common structure for different grammatical frameworks. Semantics is modeled through functorial mappings that translate syntactic structures into computational meaning using tensors, neural networks, or quantum circuits. Pragmatics is approached through the composition of these functorial models into games, allowing for the analysis of language tasks as equilibrium search problems.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The results presented in the paper are still at a preliminary stage, suggesting that further development and validation are required to establish the robustness and general applicability of the proposed diagrammatic tools for modeling language games and NLP tasks.
- While the chapter introduces lenses to capture the dynamics of probabilistic systems, the method may still struggle with fully modeling the complexity of multi-agent environments.
- Although the approach successfully captures a wide range of pragmatic scenarios, it does not yet provide a comprehensive model for all aspects of syntax, semantics, and pragmatics as highlighted through semiotics.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Develop comprehensive formal and diagrammatic tools using the theory of lenses to better model natural language processing tasks, focusing on expanding their applicability and effectiveness in pragmatic scenarios.
- Explore the use of game theory in enhancing machine learning models for natural language processing, particularly in areas such as dialogue generation and word-sense disambiguation, to improve interaction and decision-making processes.
- Investigate the integration of Bayesian pragmatics with language models to better understand speaker-listener dynamics and improve the prediction accuracy of intended referents in communication contexts.

problem_evidence.text: This thesis develops the translation between category theory and computational linguistics as a foundation for natural language processing.

method_evidence.text: This thesis develops the translation between category theory and computational linguistics as a foundation for natural language processing.

limitation_evidence.text: In parallel to its use in pragmatics, game theory has been proven significant in designing machine learning tasks. It is at the heart of multi-agent reinforcement learning [TN05] , where decision-makers interact in a stochastic environment. It is also used to improve the performance of neural network models, following the seminal work of Goodfellow et al. [Goo+14] , and is beginning to be applied to natural language processing tasks such as dialogue generation [Li+16; Li+17], knowledge graph embedding [CW18; XHW18] and word-sense disambiguation [TN19] .

The aim of this chapter is to develop formal and diagrammatic tools to model language games and NLP tasks. More precisely, we will employ the theory of lenses, which have been developed as a model for the dynamics of data-accessing programs [PGW17] and form the basis of the recent applications of category theory to both game theory [Gha+18; BHZ19] and machine learning [FST19; Cru+21] . The results are still at a preliminary stage, but the diagrammatic representation succeeds in capturing a wide range of pragmatic scenarios.

In Section 3.1, we argue that probabilistic models are best suited for analysing pragmatics and NLP tasks and show how the deterministic models studied in Chapter 2 may be turned into probabilistic ones. In Section 3.2, we introduce lenses and show how they capture the dynamics of probabilistic systems and stochastic environments. In Section 3.3, we show how parametrization may be used to capture agency in these environments and discuss the notions of optimum, best response and equilibrium for multi-agent systems. Throughout, we give examples illustrating how these concepts may be used in both pragmatics and NLP and in Section 3.4, we give a more in-depth analysis of three examples, building on recent proposals for modelling language games with category theory [HL18; de +21].

The threefold distinction between syntax, semantics and pragmatics may be traced back to the semiotics of Peirce an his trilogy between sign, object and interpretant. According to Peirce [Pei65] , these three aspects would induce three different approaches to semiotics, renewing the medieval trivium: formal grammar, logic and formal rhetoric. In his studies [Sta70] , Stalnaker gives a finer demarcation between pragmatics and semantics through the concept of context: "It is a semantic problem to specify the rules for matching up sentences of a natural language with the propositions that they express. In most cases, however, the rules will not match sentences directly with propositions, but will match sentences with propositions relative to features of the context in which the sentence is used. Those contextual features are part of the subject matter of pragmatics."

In his Philosophical Investigations [Wit53] , Wittgenstein introduces the concept of language-game (Sprachspiel ) as a basis for his theory of meaning. He never gives a general definition, and instead proceeds by enumeration of examples: "asking, thanking, cursing, greeting, praying". Thus, depending on the language-game in which it is played, the same utterance "Water!" can be interpreted as the answer to a question, a request to a waiter or the chorus of a song. From the point of view of pragmatics, language-games provide a way of capturing the notion of context, isolating a particular meaning/use of language within an environment defined by the game.

nodes.rag_limitation:
- The results presented in the paper are still at a preliminary stage, suggesting that further development and validation are required to establish the robustness and general applicability of the proposed diagrammatic tools for modeling language games and NLP tasks.
- While the chapter introduces lenses to capture the dynamics of probabilistic systems, the method may still struggle with fully modeling the complexity of multi-agent environments.
- Although the approach successfully captures a wide range of pragmatic scenarios, it does not yet provide a comprehensive model for all aspects of syntax, semantics, and pragmatics as highlighted through semiotics.
nodes.rag_method:
Categorical tools using category theory to model syntax as string diagrams, semantics using functors, and pragmatics as compositional models.

**Explanation:** Category theory offers a structured approach to model linguistic reasoning and computation. Syntax is represented with string diagrams that provide a common structure for different grammatical frameworks. Semantics is modeled through functorial mappings that translate syntactic structures into computational meaning using tensors, neural networks, or quantum circuits. Pragmatics is approached through the composition of these functorial models into games, allowing for the analysis of language tasks as equilibrium search problems.
nodes.rag_problem: The challenge of creating a unified framework for syntax, semantics, and pragmatics in natural language processing.
nodes.rag_future_work:
- Develop comprehensive formal and diagrammatic tools using the theory of lenses to better model natural language processing tasks, focusing on expanding their applicability and effectiveness in pragmatic scenarios.
- Explore the use of game theory in enhancing machine learning models for natural language processing, particularly in areas such as dialogue generation and word-sense disambiguation, to improve interaction and decision-making processes.
- Investigate the integration of Bayesian pragmatics with language models to better understand speaker-listener dynamics and improve the prediction accuracy of intended referents in communication contexts.

nodes.title: Teaching NLP with Bracelets and Restaurant Menus: An Interactive Workshop for Italian Students

deep_analysis.title: Teaching NLP with Bracelets and Restaurant Menus: An Interactive Workshop for Italian Students
deep_analysis.problem: Italian high school students lack exposure to Natural Language Processing (NLP) and computational linguistics, making responsible interaction with NLP tools and choosing this as a university discipline unlikely.
deep_analysis.method:
An interactive workshop using engaging activities like games that mimic linguistic problems faced by computers, aimed at raising awareness and interest in NLP.

**Explanation:** The workshop uses familiar concepts like restaurant menus and bracelets to illustrate complex NLP principles, showing students how language is processed computationally. By actively engaging students through problem-solving games, the workshop helps demystify computational linguistics, introduces the basics of language processing, and stimulates interest in pursuing further studies in this field.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The method lacks a formal system to collect systematic feedback, which limits the ability to assess and improve the effectiveness of the workshop.
- Time constraints pose a challenge, as longer sessions are needed to fully grasp the algorithms and have a deeper discussion on the linguistic aspects of NLP, but the current setup may not provide sufficient time.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Organize a Computational Linguistics Competition in Italy as a major project to introduce important linguistic challenges and methodologies, inspired by Radev and Pustejovsky (2013).
- Engage with a younger audience beyond middle and high-school students to raise awareness about language studies as a scientific discipline through interactive workshops.
- Develop new dissemination activities to enhance public understanding of AI-based technologies and promote critical societal awareness regarding their inner workings.
- Extend the duration of workshop sessions to allow participants a deeper understanding of the algorithms and linguistic concepts, preventing overly simplified problem-solving approaches disconnected from linguistic knowledge.

problem_evidence.text: Abstract and various sections of the paper detailing the workshop structure and goals.

method_evidence.text: Abstract and various sections of the paper detailing the workshop structure and goals.

limitation_evidence.text: For our activity, we took inspiration from one of the problems proposed in Radev and Pustejovsky (2013) . Puzzles such as the ones presented at the Computational Linguistics Competitions are a great way to introduce both important challenges and the methodology to solve them, as they stimulate students to investigate linguistic aspects in a bottomup fashion. Organizing the competition in Italy would represent a bigger project for our association, to be addressed in the coming years.

Although we did not have a formal system in place to collect systematic feedback, the overall response across venues and conditions has been extremely positive. 15 Curiosity and engagement of participants remained high both onsite and online, and we received many questions on several aspects of Natural Language Processing and neural networks, as well as concerning its role in Artificial Intelligence at large.

We want to specifically stress how time is a central ingredient in the activity. While the gamerelated aspects remained engaging and fun even in the shortened, online versions, in order to fully grasp the mechanisms underlying the presented algorithms longer sessions would be needed. In fact, we often felt that more time would be beneficial for a deeper discussion about language as an object of study itself, and about language as data on which theories can be built. In particular, shorter timeslots or less guided activities enhance the risk of participants approaching the challenge as a puzzle that they can solve regardless of linguistic knowledge. This is because the approach to language we are presenting is entirely new to our audience, and not only to the younger students.

Organizing the competition in Italy would represent a bigger project for our association, to be addressed in the coming years.

The activity described in this paper was targeted at middle to high-school students as well as the general public. It would be interesting to engage with a younger audience as well, as communicating the study and (computational) modelling of language to them would raise awareness towards language studies as a scientific discipline.

The participants' enthusiastic questions gave us many ideas for future dissemination activities. In fact, the technological world is advancing fast and we firmly believe that it is necessary to spread more awareness on the inner workings of AI-based technologies, to develop a society-level conscience to approach them in a critical way.

nodes.title: Translation Technology and Ethical Competence: An Analysis and Proposal for Translators’ Training

deep_analysis.title: Translation Technology and Ethical Competence: An Analysis and Proposal for Translators’ Training
deep_analysis.problem: Current translator training programs inadequately address the ethical implications of using advanced technologies and AI systems in translation.
deep_analysis.method:
Integrate ethical competence explicitly into translator training programs, focusing on the use of technology and AI systems.

**Explanation:** By embedding ethical discussions and guidelines within the curriculum, translator students become more aware of the potential ethical dilemmas they may encounter when using translation technologies and AI. This proactive educational approach equips them with the ability to make informed decisions and uphold ethical standards in their professional practice.
deep_analysis.limitation: 未找到明确的局限性描述
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Develop pedagogical models that integrate ethical considerations for the use of translation technology and artificial intelligence in translator training programs.
- Explore how current ethical concerns about technology in translation practice can be more effectively incorporated into teaching methodologies and curricular design.
- Assess the impact of technology-driven translation tools on the ethical decision-making process of translators, potentially guiding future curriculum adjustments.

nodes.title: Natural Language Processing for Policymaking

deep_analysis.title: Natural Language Processing for Policymaking
deep_analysis.problem: Policymakers need to analyze large amounts of textual data to derive meaningful insights for evidence-based decision making, which is traditionally tedious and time-consuming.
deep_analysis.method:
NLP offers automated methods like text classification, topic modelling, event extraction, and score prediction to efficiently process and extract vital information from text corpora.

**Explanation:** NLP methods facilitate the analysis of vast textual data by automating the extraction of categories, topics, events, and specific scores. This automates the process of identifying constituents' views, stances, and needs, providing policymakers essential data without extensive manual efforts. For example, sentiment analysis tools can gauge public opinion on issues, and topic modelling can highlight key discussions, effectively informing policy decisions.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- Our method relies heavily on the quality and comprehensiveness of the data, and there is a risk that using biased data sources, such as social media, could misrepresent public opinions, impacting important policy decisions.
- The black-box nature of modern NLP models used in our approach makes it difficult to interpret or explain the models, posing a vulnerability to adversarial attacks and making it challenging to ensure trustworthiness in decision-making systems.
- There are ethical considerations regarding the potential for misuse of NLP in policy communication, such as overly optimizing policy communication which could lead to propaganda, data privacy intrusion, or even human rights violations.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Future research could focus on improving policy communication to benefit society, addressing how language in policies can influence societal outcomes.
- There is a need to explore beyond the analysis of existing political texts, specifically aiming to use natural language processing for developing communication strategies that prioritize societal well-being over political interests.

problem_evidence.text: According to this overview, the chapter will consist of three parts. First, we introduce in Sect. 7.2 NLP methods that are applicable to political science, including text classification, topic modelling, event extraction, and score prediction.

method_evidence.text: According to this overview, the chapter will consist of three parts. First, we introduce in Sect. 7.2 NLP methods that are applicable to political science, including text classification, topic modelling, event extraction, and score prediction.

limitation_evidence.text: Apart from the limitations of the technical methodology, there are also ethical considerations arising from the use of NLP. Among the use cases introduced in this chapter, some applications of NLP are relatively safe as they mainly involve analysing public political documents and fact-based evidence or effects of policies. However, others could be concerning and vulnerable to misuse. For example, although effective, truthful policy communication is beneficial for society, it might be tempting to overdo policy communication and by all means optimize the votes. As it is highly important for government and politicians to gain positive public perception, overly optimizing policy communication might lead to propaganda, intrusion of data privacy to collect more user preferences, and, in more severe cases, surveillance and violation of human rights. Hence, there is a strong need for policies to regulate the use of technologies that influence public opinions and pose a challenge to democracy.

There are several limitations that researchers and policymakers need to take into consideration when using NLP for policymaking, due to the data-driven and black-box nature of modern NLP. First, the effectiveness of the computational models relies on the quality and comprehensiveness of the data. Although many political discourses are public, including data sources such as news, press releases, legislation, and campaigns, when it comes to surveying public opinions, social media might be a biased representation of the whole population. Therefore, when making important policy decisions, the traditional polls and surveys can provide more comprehensive coverage. Note that in the case of traditional polls, NLP can still be helpful in expediting the processing of survey answers.

The second concern is the black-box nature of modern NLP models. We do not encourage decision-making systems to depend fully on NLP, but suggest that NLP can assist human decision-makers. Hence, all the applications introduced in this chapter use NLP to compile information that is necessary for policymaking instead of directly suggesting a policy. Nonetheless, some of the models are hard to interpret or explain, such as text classification using deep learning models (Brown et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2019) , which could be vulnerable to adversarial attacks by small paraphrasing of the text input (Jin et al., 2020) . In practical applications, it is important to ensure the trustworthiness of the usage of AI. There could be a preference for transparent machine learning models if they can do the work well (e.g. LDA topic models and traditional classification methods using dictionaries or linguistic rules) or tasks with well-controlled outputs such as event extraction to select spans of the given text that mention events. In cases where only the deep learning models can provide good performance, there should be more detailed performance analysis (e.g. a study to check the correlation of the model decisions and human judgments), error analysis (e.g. different types of errors, failure modes, and potential bias towards certain groups), and studies about the interpretability of the model (e.g. feature attribution of the model, visualization of the internal states of the model).

future_work_evidence.text: Apart from analysing the language of existing political texts that aims to maximize political interests, an advanced question that is more meaningful to society is how to improve policy communication to steer towards a more beneficial future for society as a whole. There is relatively little research on this, and we welcome future work on this meaningful topic.

nodes.rag_limitation:
- Our method relies heavily on the quality and comprehensiveness of the data, and there is a risk that using biased data sources, such as social media, could misrepresent public opinions, impacting important policy decisions.
- The black-box nature of modern NLP models used in our approach makes it difficult to interpret or explain the models, posing a vulnerability to adversarial attacks and making it challenging to ensure trustworthiness in decision-making systems.
- There are ethical considerations regarding the potential for misuse of NLP in policy communication, such as overly optimizing policy communication which could lead to propaganda, data privacy intrusion, or even human rights violations.

nodes.title: Parallel implementation of machine translation using MPJ Express

deep_analysis.title: Parallel implementation of machine translation using MPJ Express
deep_analysis.problem: Machine translation systems based on tree adjoining grammar (TAG) often face performance bottlenecks due to computational intensity, especially for large datasets.
deep_analysis.method:
Parallel implementation of the machine translation system using sentence-level parallelization via MPJ Express on multicore machines and computing clusters.

**Explanation:** By implementing sentence-level parallelization, the system can distribute the computational workload across multiple cores or nodes. MPJ Express allows Java-based applications to efficiently execute tasks in parallel, thereby reducing processing time and improving scalability. This directly addresses performance bottlenecks by leveraging multicore and cluster capabilities for concurrent execution.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The method relies heavily on Java and MPJ Express for parallel implementation, which may limit performance potential compared to other programming languages and frameworks optimized for high-performance computing.
- The approach is specifically tailored for multicore machines and computing clusters, potentially reducing its applicability or efficiency on other hardware configurations or distributed systems that do not match this setup.

problem_evidence.text: We have successfully carried out sentence level parallelization and its parallel implementations on a multicore machine with varying number of cores and a computing cluster with multicore nodes.

method_evidence.text: We have successfully carried out sentence level parallelization and its parallel implementations on a multicore machine with varying number of cores and a computing cluster with multicore nodes.

limitation_evidence.text: Parallel implementation of machine translation using MPJ Express

In this paper we consider a machine translation (MT) system based on the tree adjoining grammar (TAG) formalism. We have successfully carried out sentence level parallelization and its parallel implementations on a multicore machine with varying number of cores and a computing cluster with multicore nodes. Since our code is in Java, we use MPJ Express for parallel implementations. We have carried out experiments with these parallel implementations and their performance is analysed.

nodes.title: High performance natural language processing services on the GARUDA grid

deep_analysis.title: High performance natural language processing services on the GARUDA grid
deep_analysis.problem: Natural language processing tasks require high computational power and speed, which are often constrained in traditional computing environments.
deep_analysis.method:
Parallelization of NLP components on the GARUDA grid to enhance computational performance.

**Explanation:** By utilizing the distributed computing power of the GARUDA grid and parallelizing NLP processes, the system can handle more data simultaneously. This allows for faster processing by effectively using multiple processors to divide the workload, achieving almost linear speedup, which greatly reduces execution time for complex NLP tasks compared to traditional single-processor environments.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The method's reliance on the GARUDA grid architecture may limit its applicability and generalizability to other grid or cloud computing environments where similar infrastructure is not available.
- Despite achieving a significant speedup, the method still may not achieve optimal scalability beyond 32 processors, which could hinder its performance in larger computational setups.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Improve the scalability by investigating the performance of the NLP services on a larger number of processors to achieve even greater speedup and efficiency.
- Explore the integration of additional NLP components into the existing framework to enhance the overall functionality and applicability of the service.
- Conduct experiments to optimize the parallelization techniques used on the GARUDA grid, aiming to further minimize execution time and resource usage.
- Investigate the potential application of the service in other domains beyond natural language processing, expanding its utility and impact.

nodes.title: NaturalLanguageProcesing4All

deep_analysis.title: NaturalLanguageProcesing4All
deep_analysis.problem: High school students, particularly non-programmers, face difficulties in engaging with and understanding complex Natural Language Processing (NLP) methods.
deep_analysis.method:
NLP4All, a Constructionist, low-threshold, XAI learning tool, is designed to facilitate engagement with NLP methods by enabling non-programmers to explore text corpora through classification activities.

**Explanation:** By providing a user-friendly platform that abstracts the technical complexities of NLP, NLP4All allows students to interact with NLP tasks without needing to understand the underlying programming. This makes NLP accessible, fostering curiosity and learning through hands-on exploration of real-world text data, thus bridging the gap between technology and education for non-programmers.

This makes NLP accessible, fostering curiosity and learning through hands-on exploration of real-world text data, thus bridging the gap between technology and education for non-programmers.

nodes.title: Automation of theconversion of natural language to formalized node of knowledge record

deep_analysis.title: Automation of theconversion of natural language to formalized node of knowledge record
deep_analysis.problem: Natural language sentences cannot be directly stored and utilized in a relational database for automated question answering systems.
deep_analysis.method:
The system automates the conversion of natural language sentences into a formalized node of knowledge record that can be entered into a relational database.

**Explanation:** By converting natural language into a structured format compatible with relational databases, the system allows for efficient storage and retrieval of knowledge required for question-answering. This structured storage acts as a bridge between free-form language and the rigid data structures of databases, enabling the system to understand and process queries effectively.
deep_analysis.limitation: 未找到明确的局限性描述
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Develop a web application for obtaining answers to posed questions, using the system described as a basis for implementation.
- Enhance the formal recording process of natural language sentences, enabling more efficient entry into a relational database for improved question-answering capabilities.
- Explore methods to automate the conversion of complex natural language constructs into formalized nodes of knowledge, aiming to expand the system's applicability.

nodes.title: The Structure of Meaning in Language: Parallel Narratives in Linear Algebra and Category Theory

deep_analysis.title: The Structure of Meaning in Language: Parallel Narratives in Linear Algebra and Category Theory
deep_analysis.problem: Lack of formal mathematical structure in understanding language meaning.
deep_analysis.method:
Comparison of language structures with Linear Algebra and Category Theory.

**Explanation:** By identifying parallel narratives between language and well-established mathematical frameworks, semantic structures in language can be more rigorously analyzed and understood. Linear Algebra may offer insights into the composition and transformation of language components, while Category Theory could provide an abstract framework for modeling relationships and functions within language systems.

nodes.title: “… God Said”: Toward a Quantum Theology of Creation

deep_analysis.title: “… God Said”: Toward a Quantum Theology of Creation
deep_analysis.problem: Traditional theological arguments for creation through divine speech rely heavily on divine revelation, making them difficult to support solely through observation and scientific inquiry.
deep_analysis.method:
The use of Quantum Natural Language Processing (QNLP) as a framework to demonstrate the quantum-native nature of language, allowing creation through speech to be inferred through quantum computing methods without requiring divine revelation.

**Explanation:** By establishing a mathematical system to model grammar and leveraging QNLP, the paper argues that both natural phenomena and grammar can be understood through the same abstraction—quantum circuits. This makes it possible to visualize and scientifically infer the creation of the world through speech by aligning the linguistic phenomenon with observable quantum interactions, thus bypassing the need for divine revelation.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The approach relies heavily on prior research and background knowledge, which may limit accessibility and understanding for those not already familiar with the foundational concepts and theories.
- The method assumes a metaphysical perspective that may not be universally accepted, potentially alienating those who do not share the same philosophical or theological viewpoints.
- Although the work suggests that no divine revelation is required to observe and participate in the creation through speech, it may still face challenges in convincing secular audiences given its connection with religious narratives.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Explore the development of a quantum theistic interpretive framework that expands the initial argument for creation via quantum linguistic phenomena, focusing on the possible implications of theism for understanding the quantum-native nature of language.
- Expand the initial category theory-based representation of theistic cosmology to incorporate specific theological nuances, such as sacraments and the effects of spiritual acts like baptism, using symbolic and ritualistic elements instantiated from 'heaven-stuff' and 'earth-stuff'.
- Develop mathematical and philosophical models to accurately represent the participation and interconnectedness of all creation within a quantum universe, integrating insights from Christian mystical traditions and quantum physics.
- Investigate the implications of quantum mechanics and category theory for abstraction processes in natural theology, aiming to model the characteristics of a Creator as revealed through the natural world and understand the real existence of abstract objects.

problem_evidence.text: This work argues that because Quantum Mechanics (QM) is superb at simulating natural, physical phenomena, God's generative language of physical creation is native to a quantum world; therefore, it is now possible to postulate an argument for God's creation of the world through language without requiring divine revelation.

method_evidence.text: This work argues that because Quantum Mechanics (QM) is superb at simulating natural, physical phenomena, God's generative language of physical creation is native to a quantum world; therefore, it is now possible to postulate an argument for God's creation of the world through language without requiring divine revelation.

limitation_evidence.text: Next theology and philosophy enter the picture. First, assuming natural theologythe idea that the natural world reveals the characteristics of the Creatoris a given. If the qualities of the Creator cannot be seen in the creation, any enterprise searching for truth through scientific means, or simply the senses in general is doomed to failure. Next, if the natural world reveals the qualities of the Creator, then abstraction is the process by which the qualities of the Creator may be ascertained. By examining the Augustinian proof for the existence of God, it becomes evident that a form of scholastic realism provides the best explanation for the existence of abstract objectsthe outputs of the abstraction processand their independence from created minds. There must be a supreme mind that holds these abstract objects in existence apart from the existence and experience of human consciousness. Whether that is the God of Christianity, another religion, or consciousness itself is immaterial for this portion of the argument. All that matters is that abstract objects have some kind of real existence.

A specific quantum theology begins to emerge at this point. Theoretical quantum physics demonstrates the interconnectedness of the world so that everything is a participant in the ongoing unfolding of reality. The implication here is that classical experimentation is quite limited in its explanatory power, and is, certainly, metaphysically useless when it comes to discerning the nature of the world since it neither accurately reflects the nature of the world nor its structure. Furthermore, classical experimentation creates the great sin of separation. Creation exists as a great orchestra of resounding unity, and any attempt to dissect it into its individual parts creates a skewed view of the world. Unity is where the world is seen as functioning in its true state, and in this unity is where understanding becomes possible. The forest can no longer be lost for the trees. The great mystics of all traditions were aware of this shamanic truth. Christian mystics are chief among these as contemplation of the eternal relationship of the Trinity has led them to the understanding that all of creation exists in relationship with itself, with the Creator, and with humanity. To be, whether abstract objects or material objects, is to be in a relationship. Because it is now wrestling with the quantum realm, science is now beginning to catch up to the Christian mystical tradition.

Finally, the inquisitive mind returns to the quantum circuits and takes a moment to contemplate a couple of examples: one representing a physical process, and another representing grammatical, human language. It is at this point that the Christian account of the Creator begins to be the most fitting, for the Christian God is one that exists in eternal relationshipperhaps eternal entanglementand creates through the spoken word, that is the Wordthe Logos. Any secular philosopher and any theologian alike seeks to understand the structure of reality and the underlying principlethe Logosthat makes it understandable. It is the very fact that the material order and the spoken order are really the same order that one observesreally participates inin the quantum circuits with no divine revelation required. This is that principle. It is in this counterintuitive space, opened by viewing the outputs of a scientific experiment with a metaphysical mind, that the creation of the material and the creation of the grammatical as ascended into the same abstraction become interchangeable: "Were you there when I laid the foundation of grammar?". 69 It is in this space that the continued speech of God creates and holds all lovingly in being even now, where the dancing of our electrons and the beats of our hearts answer Him.  69 Entrustedtothedirt, "Wonder of Language;" Job 38:4.

From the outset, this work has endeavored to show primarily that QNLP has a unique theological and philosophical ramification in that it allows the concept of creation through speech in accordance with the Logos, divinely revealed in Genesis 1 and John 1, to be inferredeven observed and participated withvisibly from the discipline of quantum computing alone. No divine revelation is required. This is the principal contribution of this work. There is, however, a great deal of background and prior research required to substantiate this contribution.

It starts, in the strictly scientific sense, with the fact that the world is not classical. It is instead quantum mechanical, which brings with it a certain set of metaphysical claims as to the nature of reality and the fact that the very act of observation itself is a participation in the experiment. There are no longer subjects or objects, only participants. Further, since the world is quantum mechanical, the underlying mathematics of the universe are also quantum. This mathematics can be described in two camps: quantum mechanics itselfthe nuts and bolts of matrix mathematics, Kronecker products, and other approachesand category theory. The former leads to the development of quantum simulation and circuits and the latter to an abstraction fit for describing many disciplines and realities within a singular, higher-order mathematical abstraction. Category theory is itself strong enough to model the theistic universe insofar as it provides a framework for  68 Whitfield et al., "Electronic Structure Hamiltonians."

This argument, then, abductively implies that theism is a possible explanation for the quantum-native nature of language and that further development of a quantum theistic interpretive framework is an avenue for further research toward which this endeavor merely points. Additionally, as a necessary consequence of the first contribution, a second novel contribution presents an initial step toward a category theory-based representation of theistic cosmologya universe created out of heaven-stuff and earth-stuff. 9 This initial step can be expanded to fuller representations that account for specific theological nuances, such as sacraments and sacramentals, effects of sin or baptism on the soul, and so on, in future works.

nodes.title: Recognizing Hate with NLP: The Teaching Experience of the #DeactivHate Lab in Italian High Schools

deep_analysis.title: Recognizing Hate with NLP: The Teaching Experience of the #DeactivHate Lab in Italian High Schools
deep_analysis.problem: Young generations lack awareness and tools to effectively recognize and mitigate the impact of online hate speech.
deep_analysis.method:
Development and implementation of an annotation platform for Italian tweets used in the #DeactivHate laboratory targeting secondary school students.

**Explanation:** The annotation platform allows students to engage directly with real-world examples of hate speech on social media, enhancing their ability to identify and understand such behavior. By providing a structured environment where students can annotate tweets, the platform educates them on recognising hate speech nuances, thereby raising awareness and empowering them to mitigate its impact. This hands-on experience is designed to foster critical thinking and encourage proactive responses to online hate speech.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The annotation platform implemented for Italian tweets may not generalize well to other languages or cultural contexts, limiting its applicability beyond the specific setting of Italian high schools.
- Our method may struggle to accurately identify and classify nuanced forms of hate speech that are context-dependent, particularly those that involve subtle or implicit language cues.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Exploring the adaptation of the annotation platform to detect hate speech in other languages and cultural contexts could be a future direction, broadening its applicability and effectiveness globally.
- Developing additional educational content and training modules that incorporate the latest advancements in NLP for educators and students can enhance the #DeactivHate Lab's resources and impact.
- Investigating the long-term effects of using the #DeactivHate Lab's tools and curriculum on students’ understanding and behavior towards online hate speech would provide valuable insights and guide future improvements.

nodes.title: A dissemination workshop for introducing young Italian students to NLP

deep_analysis.title: A dissemination workshop for introducing young Italian students to NLP
deep_analysis.problem: Young Italian students lack exposure to and understanding of Natural Language Processing (NLP), hindering their awareness and interest in the field.
deep_analysis.method:
A modular game-based dissemination workshop with interactive hands-on activities designed to simulate NLP processes.

**Explanation:** The workshop uses games inspired by word puzzles and involves participants acting as computers to rearrange tokens according to NLP principles. This engaging format demystifies NLP concepts by illustrating the computational approach to language processing in an accessible manner. By physically manipulating sentence tokens and extracting grammatical rules, students can intuitively grasp the challenges and methods of NLP, increasing their interest and understanding of the field through active participation.
deep_analysis.limitation:
- The preparation of the activity involves several manual steps, such as manually selecting words for the card decks, cutting and gluing sentences on transparent supports, and producing strips of felt or threads, which can be time-consuming and labor-intensive.
- The overall process requires specific software and dependencies, such as Python 3.6 or above, lualatex, pdftk, and pdfnup, which might pose accessibility issues for individuals unfamiliar with these tools or lacking the necessary technical setup.
deep_analysis.future_work:
- Explore more efficient methods for preparing and distributing teaching materials to reduce the manual workload, especially in creating printable formats and translating content.
- Enhance the accessibility of the Python scripts and other technical requirements to support a wider range of operating environments, possibly including online platforms for easier user access.
- Investigate collaborations with additional educational and scientific institutions to expand the reach and impact of the NLP workshop, leveraging their resources and expertise.
- Develop an open web interface to facilitate interactive participation in the workshop, potentially increasing engagement from students outside Italy and in diverse learning environments.

problem_evidence.text: Our interactive workshops took the form of modular games where participants, guided by trained tutors, acted as if they were computers that had to recognize speech and text, as well as to generate written sentences in a mysterious language they knew nothing about.

method_evidence.text: Our interactive workshops took the form of modular games where participants, guided by trained tutors, acted as if they were computers that had to recognize speech and text, as well as to generate written sentences in a mysterious language they knew nothing about.

limitation_evidence.text: The preparation of the activity consists of several steps: (1) creating and tagging the corpora with morpho-syntactic and syntactic categories as described in the repository; (2) choosing the words to include in the card decks: these must be manually selected but scripts are provided to generate possible sentences based on bigram co-occurrences, and to extract all the possible grammar rules present in the annotation; (3) when the produced sentences and grammar are satisfactory, scripts are provided to generate (i) the printable formats of corpora and decks of cards, (ii) a dictionary to support the translation of sentences in the last part of the workshop, and (iii) clear-text corpora; (4) sentences from the clear-text corpora have to be manually cut and glued on a transparent support that can be superimposed on the printed corpora to reveal the sentences; (5) finally, some manual work is necessary: producing strips of felt or any material with the same colors used in the corpus; cutting threads; attaching a button loop to the relevant cards, etc.

In the spirit of open science and to encourage the popularization of NLP, the teaching materials and source code are freely available in our repository (see footnotes 2-3 for the links). The print-ready material is released under CC BY-NC; the source code is distributed under the GNU gpl license version 3. All scripts work for Python versions 3.6 or above and the overall process requires python3, lualatex, pdftk and pdfnup as detailed in the README.md file in the repository, which contains all necessary instructions.

We would like to thank the board of the Italian Association for Computational Linguistics (AILC) for the support given to the workshop development and delivery. We are also grateful to BergamoScienza, Festival della Scienza di Genova, Science Web Festival for hosting the activity during the festivals; to ILC-CNR "Antonio Zampolli " and ColingLab (University of Pisa) for hosting us during the European Night of Research; and to the Scuola Internazionale Superiore di Studi Avanzati (SISSA) and ITI Tullio Buzzi for hosting our activities with their students. We also thank Dr. Mirko Lai , who has collaborated on the development of the web interface for the online versions of our activity.
